listings.title: A History Of The Maxwell & Nukumaru  Districts Rural Families
listings.body:
This 1998 publication by George Abbott provides an extensive historical record of the Maxwell and Nukumaru districts. The text traces the region's transformation from the earliest Polynesian and European discoveries through the Land Wars to the development of a thriving rural community.

Key historical features include:
* Early Origins: Detailed accounts of Polynesian discoverers, including Kupe, followed by the arrivals of Tasman and Cook.
* Conflict & Resettlement: Documentation of the looming Land Wars and the subsequent return of settlers to the district.
* Local Development: History of the district's founder, Pharazyn, and the evolution of farming productivity and communications.
* Education & Community: Comprehensive sagas of the Maxwell School and the 47-year history of the Nukumaru School.
* Geographic Records: Features maps of unformed roads, district layouts from 1910 and 1939, and aerial photographic inserts.

Selected Table of Contents:
* Geology Of The District
* The Polynesian Discoverers & The First Europeans
* Land Wars Loom & The Settlers Return
* Maxwell Founder Pharazyn
* Early Farm Productivity
* The Maxwell and Nukumaru School Sagas

listings.title: Tutira A History - Maori History & Early Settlers 1951 edition With Corrections
listings.body:
Tutira  A History  + Maori History And Early Settlers

In 1880, William Herbert Guthrie-Smith (1862-1940) emigrated from Scotland to New Zealand, where he learned the basics of sheep farming and acquired Tutira, a disused sheep station of 20, 000 acres in the Hawkes Bay region of the North Island. 

Tutira, published in 1953, describes every aspect of Guthrie-Smiths enterprise, including the redevelopment of the land and comprehensive advice on sheep farming. 

The book also covers the history of the local Maori and of European settlement, and provides an extensive account of the farms natural history including its geological configuration, meteorological patterns, the formation of lakes and waterways, and the native plant and bird species Guthrie-Smith discovered on his land.

 It also draws attention to the impact of introduced, alien plants and animals. "

This comprehensive regional history provides a factual account of the development of the Horowhenua County from its early Māori inhabitants through to the mid-20th century. Written by Anthony Dreaver, this book serves as a documented record of the social, economic, and political shifts within the district.

The body of the work examines the transition from dense bushland to productive pastoral land, focusing on the impact of the Wellington-Manawatu railway on local settlement. Detailed sections cover the growth of key townships including Levin, Shannon, and Tokomaru, while also providing data on local infrastructure, such as the establishment of the Horowhenua County Council and the drainage of local swamplands.

It includes extensive lists of early settlers, local officials, and those who served in the world wars. It also documents the development of local industries including flax milling, timber, and dairy farming. Factual accounts of the various schools, churches, and community organisations that formed the backbone of the rural districts are provided throughout.

listings.title: Te Kao 75 Year History Of The Area The School And Its People
listings.body:
Te Kao: 75 Year History Of The Area The School And Its People provides an account of the development of the Te Kao district in the Far North. This serves as a vital record of the social, educational, and cultural growth of the community, documenting the transition of the region from its early pioneer foundations to the established settlement of the mid-twentieth century.

The text offers significant genealogical value, recording the families and personalities who shaped the Te Kao Area School and the surrounding district. It documents the essential role of the school as a community hub, tracing the history of local education alongside the broader development of infrastructure and land use in the Aupouri Peninsula. This record is a primary reference for researchers interested in the unique heritage of the Far North and the resilient spirit of the people of Te Kao.

This publication is highly regarded for its inclusion of archival documentation, school committee records, and historical accounts that capture the daily life and milestones of the district. It provides an accurate record of the local institutions and social organisations that fostered a strong regional identity throughout the 75-year period covered.

listings.title: Scandinavians Settling in Wairarapa Manawatu HBay
listings.body:
SCANDINAVIAN FOOTPRINTS – a story of Scandinavians settling in New Zealand – Revised Edition

Very good condition Card cover 319 pages, black and white photos

By Margit Brew

Published in 2007

With the economic depression of the 1870s stalling progress, the New Zealand Government turned to Scandinavia for help. 

Swedes, Norwegians, and Danes (lumbermen, farmers and carpenters) arrived to take up the challenge of clearing bush from Wairarapa, Manawatu, and Hawkes Bay so roads and railways could be built.

In 1866, Swede Bror Erik Friberg and his young wife arrive to this scene and Bror Erik was soon appointed by the government to act as an agent with a focus on the Scandinavian settlers in Hawkes Bay.

Margit Brew tells of Bror Erik's early life in Sweden and the concerns for the difficulties his countrymen face. Having arrived heavily in debt to the government these immigrants strive to survive in a country where nobody speaks their language and where there is plenty of sickness and little or no medical care.

Scandinavians in New Zealand left footprints by way of a rich legacy, particularly in New Zealand’s dairying and agriculture industries, As Vikings they were true trail blazers

listings.title: Travels of Hochstetter and Haast in New Zealand 1858  -  1860
listings.body:
Travels of Hochstetter and Haast in New Zealand 1858  -  1860

338 pages - Ex Library but VG condition

Hardback VG condition He arrived in December 1858 as geologist on the Austrian Novara Expedition, at a time when much of the British colony was little known to science. Hochstetter was soon asked to investigate the vast hinterland of Auckland province and, after that, Nelson’s too.

            In early 1859, Hochstetter, with his countryman Julius Haast, travelled by foot, horseback and canoe deep into the North Island’s interior to the volcanic zone. Hochstetter recorded a dramatic account of all he saw, including the remarkable Pink and White Terraces. He also vividly documented his encounters with many Maori along the way, from high chief to mailman. Whether describing his mediating between warring clans or arguments over Sunday observance and wedding feast protocols, he delivered an engaging account of Maori life in the Waikato in the last years of peace.
 Hochstetter also documented the country’s unusual flora and fauna. Some of the many new species he collected were named after him, including a giant land snail and a primitive frog. In Nelson he obtained the first complete skeletons of the giant extinct moa, as well as a rock new to science from Dun Mountain, which he named dunite. His collaboration with Haast proved even more fruitful, and on Hochstetter’s endorsement his friend was appointed leader of an arduous expedition to the West Coast in 1860

This 47-page family history, published in 1990, documents the genealogy and pioneering life of the Lambert family in the Hawke's Bay region. The primary focus is on Alfred Lambert (1820–1900) and his wife Sarah, who emigrated from Ardleigh, Essex, England.  

Alfred arrived in Wellington in 1853 aboard the Cornwall, with Sarah and their young son, Alfred Ocean, joining him the following year. The couple initially lived in a mud and raupo hut near Porangahau and were instrumental in the early settlement of the Wimbledon district.  

The text provides clinical details on the family's sheep farming trials and their connections to other prominent local names such as the Saint-Hill, Chapman, and Hales families. It includes information on their eight children: Alfred Ocean, Thomas Gersham, Henry Arthur, Mary Ann Eliza, Jemima Amelia (who married into the Hales family), Eleanor Jane, Eunice Maud, and Charlotte Maria.

listings.title: History Of Irish Settlers In New Zealand 246 pages
listings.body:
A Lucky Landing

Good++++

They travelled across the world in search of a new life, a piece of land to call their own. They came, too, in their thousands, to dig gold. 

Many of the young women came to work as servants in the homes of those more wealthy than themselves; the men often earned a living building bridges, tunnels and railways until they could afford a farm of their own. Some came as priests and nuns to teach and care for their fellow countrymen and women. 

The Irish who poured into NZ, particularly as assisted immigrants in the last third of the 19th century, have left an indelible mark of this country's history- and on its people: it is estimated that an astonishing 20 per cent of NZ's population can claim Irish ancestry. A Lucky Landing tells of the colourful Irish men and women who have played a part in NZ's politics, in its literature, in its commercial success

There are chapters on the Irish goldminers, on the battles between Orange and Green that were once fought fiercely in this country, on the Irish influence on the Catholic church, on the many Irish who became publicans or policemen, and on the discrimination suffered by the Irish immigrants in the days before being Irish was a matter of pride

Indexed with names 246 pages softcovered

listings.title: Rangiwaea Waiouru School Jubilee History Memories Reunion Events + Local Stories
listings.body:
Rangiwaea Waiouru School Jubilee History Memories Reunion Events + Local Stories

This Jubilee history records the people, places and memories connected with Rangiwaea and the community that grew around its school. The booklet brings together recollections, historical notes and reunion material that celebrate the shared experiences of former pupils, teachers and families. The opening pages include welcome messages and introductions that set the tone for a gathering focused on connection and remembrance. The Jubilee Committee is listed with the names of those who organised the event and the programme outlines the activities held on Saturday 5 April and Sunday 6 April 1980. These included assembly, the official opening, roll call, photographs, commemorative tree planting, afternoon tea, cocktails, dinner, a thanksgiving service and a final lunch and farewells. The contents are arranged into four parts that explore the history of the district, the development of the school and the stories of those who attended it. The historical section describes the early settlement of the area and the establishment of the school as a focal point for the community. Recollections from former pupils and teachers provide insight into classroom life, playground traditions and the character of rural schooling. The book also includes accounts of local families, community events and the role of the school as a gathering place. The later sections present memories from different decades and highlight the changes that shaped the district over time.  

Condition Good+++  60 pages with photos

Covers the fever-pitch first three years of the Otago rush: Gabriel’s Gully, Tuapeka, Fox’s, the Dunstan, and the wild winter exodus to the Lake Wakatipu diggings.
Imagine the Kawarau Gorge minus today’s highway guard-rails: river 40 m below, wagon wheels on ice, £3 worth of flour your entire fortune. That’s the tightrope Glasson calls the “golden cobweb.” His prose can feel quaint, but the raw stakes still thrum. Borrow it, brew a strong tea, and time-travel to the moment Otago went from sleepy pastoral backwater to Asia-Pacific boomtown.

Need more Otago rush lore or modern hiking routes that trace the pack-tracks?....  Just holler—plenty more veins to work

Hardback First Edition 1957

Piriaka the story of a Main Trunk settlement 1901-1981 is a commemorative souvenir booklet produced for the Piriaka School 70th Jubilee. The text provides a factual chronological history of the settlement located in the King Country region of New Zealand. It documents the industrial and social development of the district from the opening of the King Country in the early 1900s through to the jubilee celebrations in 1981.

* Early Settlement: Records the arrival of the railway and its role in the establishment of the town including the early town plan and postal services.
* Industrial Infrastructure: Provides factual data regarding the commencement of bushfelling in the 1900s sawmill sites and the development of the local dairy factory which served the area for 64 years.
* Local Utilities: Documents the construction of the power station in 1923 and the establishment of the regional abattoir.
* Educational History: Traces the history of Piriaka School from 1903 to 1907 and its subsequent growth including the teacher roll call and former pupils.
* Social & Community Life: Records the activities of the Maori Women’s Welfare League sport and social life and the decade-by-decade groups of the district.
* Jubilee Commemoration: Detailed accounts of the 70th Jubilee celebrations including the committee and the status of former residents in 1981.

It utilizes official records and personal interviews with former residents to provide a factual account of the community's evolution. It further documents the development of the swimming baths and aviary as part of the town’s social infrastructure. This booklet is a central reference for genealogical and regional research into the Piriaka and Manunui districts.

listings.title: Miranui The story of Largest flax Mill Southern Press Shannon Horowhenua History
listings.body:
Miranui (Maori for "big mill") was a pioneering, large-scale flax mill operating from 1907 to 1933 near Shannon, Horowhenua, and was arguably the largest in the Southern Hemisphere. Owned by the A&L Seifert Flaxdressing Company, it produced up to 2,500 tons of fibre annually and was a major engine of the local economy.

Opened in November 1907 by Alfred and Louis Seifert, it was the first mill specially designed for massive-scale flax fibre production from the Makerua Swamp.Operation: The mill was located 2.5 miles north of Shannon on the Shannon-Tokomaru road (modern Highway 57). It processed Phormium tenax (harakeke) from the 22,000-acre Makerua swamp.Peak Production: By 1916–17, 19 mills operated in the area, with Miranui employing 300 men at its peak. It had seven stripping machines.Industrial Innovation: The mill was famous for its 3ft gauge tramway, which ran 3.5 miles into the swamp to bring flax to the machines.

Today, almost all evidence of the mill is gone, with the swamp converted into dairy farmland.The only remaining trace is the ruins of the scutching shed.The area is still known as the Opiki Plains, and the site of the mill is on the Shannon-Tokomaru road

Author: Bob Ayson.Publisher: Southern Press.Published: 1977.


 This 24-page booklet documents the history of the Seifert brothers' mill, the flax processing method, and the surrounding community

Good+++

Hardback 2nd Edition 1967 150 pages


This book is a simple biography in the form of re-miniscences, of "Bob Mc-Latchie," a widely known pioneer, and the authors own father. His parents came out to Milton from Scotland in the 1850's - 1860's and the real meat of the story lies in his winning by ballot a block of virgin bush in the Owaka district, when the Seddon Government was breaking in new lands.

Although there are many facts and incidents recorded, the real value of the book lies in its authenticity as a social document. Here in detail is an account of the primitive beginnings of a New Zealand farm. As a teenager, McLatchie carried his first supplies into the bush, felled his first clear-ing, came to know and under-stand the natural life of the rain forest, and by tremen-dous personal effort changed a rotting forest on the hills into a productive farm. There is an incredible simplicity about it all. One man against the giant trees, finding food with a gun, creating a warm fellowship with others scattered through the bush.

listings.title: Palmerston North Technical School Golden Anniversary 1906-1956 Souvenir Booklet
listings.body:
PALMERSTON NORTH TECHNICAL SCHOOL 1906 - 1956

This Golden Anniversary Souvenir Booklet documents the fifty-year history of the Palmerston North Technical School in the Manawatu region, NZ. It records the institution's development from its founding in 1906 through to its jubilee celebrations in 1956. The content includes a chronological account of the school's growth, detailing the expansion of technical and vocational education within the local community. It features lists of principals, staff members, and board of governors who served during the first five decades. The text describes the various courses offered, from trade training to commercial subjects, and the role the school played in the regional economy. Included are archival photographs of the school buildings, classrooms, and student groups across different eras. It records the activities of the Old Pupils' Association and various sporting and cultural clubs associated with the school. The publication provides insight into the educational standards and social life of students in mid-century NZ. It serves as a genealogical and historical resource for those researching the heritage of technical education in Palmerston North. The cover displays the school crest and motto, "Scientia Est Potentia," marking its 50th year of service.
